documents.content: Compensation negotiation lessons from negotiating four offers in late 2024: always have competing offers, even soft ones from earlier-stage processes. Companies negotiate harder when they know they're competing. Always negotiate after the offer arrives, never during the loop — premature money discussions can sink an offer. Counter on total compensation, not just base salary. The negotiable levers in order of typical flexibility: signing bonus (most flexible, doesn't affect ongoing comp budgets), stock grant (moderately flexible, especially at refresh time), base salary (least flexible, ties to band/level). Get every offer in writing before resigning your current role. Verbal offers can and do disappear.

Specific negotiation phrases that work without being adversarial: 'I'm excited about this role. Based on the other offers I'm considering and the impact I expect to have here, I was hoping we could get to X total compensation. Is there flexibility on the signing bonus or stock component?' This frames it as collaborative problem-solving, not confrontation. If the recruiter says 'this is our best offer', that's almost never true — politely ask if they can check with the hiring manager. Don't make ultimatums you're not willing to walk away from. Don't lie about competing offers — recruiters sometimes verify and it always ends badly. Send a thank-you note after every conversation, regardless of outcome. Recruiting communities are small and reputations matter.
